metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan that `metrics.py` must import `rebar.metrics` (the package, not just `rebar.metrics.registry`) to trigger the `event_metrics`/`git_metrics`/`sidecar_metrics` side-effect registration, and that the AC test must import REGISTRY from `rebar.metrics` (not `rebar.metrics.registry`) to catch any under-registration.
evidence: src/rebar/metrics/__init__.py:18 — `from rebar.metrics import event_metrics, git_metrics, sidecar_metrics  # noqa: E402,F401` is the side-effect import that populates REGISTRY beyond the 3 seed specs
src/rebar/metrics/registry.py:133-158 — REGISTRY at import time of registry.py contains only 3 seed MetricSpecs using `_no_data_yet`
src/rebar/metrics/event_metrics.py:372, git_metrics.py:280, sidecar_metrics.py:292 — each module calls `register()` at module level, appending specs only when the module is imported

Plan text: 'reaches metrics only through rebar.metrics.registry (and the package import that populates REGISTRY)' — the parenthetical is ambiguous about which import (rebar.metrics vs rebar.metrics.registry) does the populating
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `metrics.py` reaches a fully-populated REGISTRY by importing 'through `rebar.metrics.registry` (and the package import that populates REGISTRY)', but `rebar.metrics.registry` alone contains only the 3 seed specs with `_no_data_yet` computes. The git/event/sidecar metrics are only appended to REGISTRY as side-effects of importing `rebar.metrics` (the package `__init__.py` imports `event_metrics`, `git_metrics`, `sidecar_metrics` which each call `register()`). If `metrics.py` imports from `rebar.metrics.registry` directly without also importing `rebar.metrics` (or triggering the package `__init__`), it will receive an incomplete REGISTRY — and the AC test `set(output['metrics'].keys()) >= {s.id for s in REGISTRY}` would trivially pass against the truncated seed set rather than the full populated registry.
findings.location: Approach section — 'it builds a context object and calls rebar.metrics.registry.evaluate(spec, context) for every MetricSpec' / 'reaches metrics only through rebar.metrics.registry (and the package import that populates REGISTRY)'
scenarios: metrics.py does `from rebar.metrics.registry import REGISTRY, evaluate` — gets only 3 seed specs; AC test passes vacuously because it asserts against this same truncated REGISTRY
An operator runs `rebar metrics` and gets output for only 3 metrics instead of the full set including git/event/sidecar derivations
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach section, replace the parenthetical with: 'metrics.py imports `from rebar.metrics import REGISTRY, evaluate` (the package `__init__` triggers the reader side-effects); the AC test similarly imports REGISTRY via `from rebar.metrics import REGISTRY` so it sees the fully-populated set.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the `_cli/__init__.py` edit instruction that `metrics` needs a direct `if sub == 'metrics':` arm in `_dispatch()` (or as an intercept in `_main_dispatch()`), NOT membership in an existing frozenset — every existing frozenset routes to a shared backend incompatible with a custom `metrics.py` handler.
evidence: Every frozenset in `_cli/__init__.py` routes to a pre-existing shared handler: `_READS_INIT_ONLY` → `reads.main([sub, *rest])`, `_WRITES_FULL` → `commands_main([sub, *rest])`, etc.
`_dispatch()` line 329: 'raise RuntimeError(f"rebar: subcommand {sub!r} is known but has no in-process handler")' — reached when a subcommand has a help file but no dispatch arm.
Analogous novel commands (`audit`, `config`, `grounding-info`, `fsck`) each use direct `if sub == '...'` arms in `_main_dispatch()` or `_dispatch()`, NOT frozenset membership.
ABSENCE: no frozenset routes to a dispatch-to-module-by-name pattern that `metrics.py` could plug into.
findings.finding: The plan instructs the implementer to 'register the `metrics` read subcommand in the command-set frozensets' in `_cli/__init__.py`, but no existing frozenset accommodates a novel command with a custom handler: every frozenset routes to a shared backend (`reads.main`, `commands_main`, etc.), and adding `metrics` to any of them (e.g. `_READS_INIT_ONLY`) would route it to that wrong backend. The correct mechanism is a direct `if sub == 'metrics':` arm in `_dispatch()` (matching how `audit`, `config`, `grounding-info` etc. are handled), not a frozenset entry.
findings.location: Files section: `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py` — 'register the metrics read subcommand in the command-set frozensets and dispatch'
scenarios: An implementer adds `metrics` to `_READS_INIT_ONLY`: dispatch calls `reads.main(['metrics', ...])`, which does not handle that subcommand, causing a crash or wrong exit.
An implementer adds `metrics` to `_READS_NO_INIT` for the same reason — same failure mode.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'register the `metrics` read subcommand in the command-set frozensets' with: 'add a direct `if sub == "metrics":` arm in `_dispatch()` that imports and calls `rebar._commands.metrics.metrics_cli(rest)` — matching the pattern used by `fsck`, `delete`, and other novel commands rather than using an existing frozenset.'

findings.finding: The plan's Approach says metrics.py 'reaches metrics only through rebar.metrics.registry (and the package import that populates REGISTRY)', but this is ambiguous: importing rebar.metrics.registry alone does NOT trigger the register() side-effect calls in git_metrics, event_metrics, or sidecar_metrics — those are triggered only by importing the rebar.metrics package (__init__.py line 18). A developer who writes `from rebar.metrics.registry import REGISTRY, evaluate` will get only the 3 seed specs in REGISTRY, not the 9 reader-registered specs, making AC1 fail silently (the map will have only 3 entries, not all registered ids).
findings.location: Approach section, paragraph 1: '(and the package import that populates REGISTRY)'; AC1
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Approach: 'metrics.py must `import rebar.metrics` (the package __init__) before iterating REGISTRY, so that git_metrics.register(), event_metrics.register(), and sidecar_metrics.register() have fired and all specs are present. Importing only rebar.metrics.registry skips those side effects and leaves REGISTRY with only the 3 seed specs.'

findings.finding: The plan instructs `metrics.py` to import only `rebar.metrics.registry` (not `rebar.metrics`), but the full REGISTRY is only populated after `rebar.metrics.__init__` runs — which imports event_metrics, git_metrics, and sidecar_metrics as side effects. Importing `rebar.metrics.registry` directly yields only the 3 seed stubs, so the command would dispatch against an incomplete REGISTRY and the AC coverage assertion would silently validate only the stubs.
findings.location: Approach section: 'it builds a context object and calls rebar.metrics.registry.evaluate(spec, context) for every MetricSpec' / 'metrics.py reaches metrics only through rebar.metrics.registry'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the approach text to: 'metrics.py imports `rebar.metrics` (the package) so that __init__.py triggers event_metrics/git_metrics/sidecar_metrics registrations as side effects; it then iterates over `rebar.metrics.registry.REGISTRY`. It must NOT import rebar.metrics.adapters — the source-level guard remains.'

findings.finding: The REGISTRY hydration import protocol is under-specified: the plan says `metrics.py` imports through `rebar.metrics.registry` but the full REGISTRY (including event/git/sidecar metrics) is only populated when `rebar.metrics` (the package `__init__`) is imported, not `rebar.metrics.registry` alone — an implementor following the plan's wording may import only the registry module and get a 3-entry stub REGISTRY.
findings.location: Approach section: 'reaches metrics only through `rebar.metrics.registry` (and the package import that populates `REGISTRY`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Approach: 'metrics.py must `import rebar.metrics` (not just `rebar.metrics.registry`) so that event_metrics/git_metrics/sidecar_metrics register() side-effects run and REGISTRY is fully hydrated before the render loop iterates it.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `registry.evaluate` (or the per-metric dispatch in `metrics.py`) wraps each reader's `compute` call in an isolated try/except and maps any exception to an `unavailable` entry (with `reason: "reader_error"` or similar), so one bad reader cannot crash the full command.
evidence: Plan says 'emitting value-or-unavailable per metric' but does not specify whether reader exceptions are caught and converted to unavailable or allowed to propagate
No mention of try/except isolation around per-metric evaluate calls
The --bucket path evaluates the registry once per sub-range, compounding the risk: one reader exception aborts the entire bucketed series
findings.finding: The plan does not specify whether `registry.evaluate` (or the CLI loop in `metrics.py`) catches exceptions thrown by individual reader `compute` functions and converts them to `unavailable`, or allows them to propagate and crash the entire CLI invocation. If a single misbehaving reader throws an unhandled exception, the user loses all output for all metrics.
findings.location: Approach — 'builds a context object and calls rebar.metrics.registry.evaluate(spec, context) for every MetricSpec, emitting value-or-unavailable per metric'
scenarios: A reader hits a filesystem permission error mid-series during --bucket evaluation, crashing the CLI with a traceback instead of partial results
A newly registered metric's compute function has a bug; the user gets no output from rebar metrics at all instead of unavailable for that one metric
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach section, add: 'Each call to a reader's compute is wrapped individually; any exception is caught and emitted as `{"unavailable": {"reason": "reader_error: <exc type>", "accruing_since": null}}` so all other metrics still render.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the Approach (and reflect in `metrics.py` / `evaluate`) that exceptions raised by `compute` are caught and converted to an `Unavailable` result (with the exception message as the reason), so a failing reader never aborts the full evaluation loop.
evidence: Plan: 'calls `rebar.metrics.registry.evaluate(spec, context)` for every `MetricSpec`, emitting value-or-`unavailable` per metric'
registry.py evaluate(): `result = spec.compute(context)` — no try/except block
AC1 asserts every id appears in the output map, implying no metric may be silently dropped — yet a raised exception would drop all remaining metrics
No plan text addresses exception handling in compute callables
findings.finding: The plan specifies that `metrics.py` emits 'value-or-unavailable per metric' for every registered id, but never specifies the fallback when a reader's `compute` callable raises an exception. The existing `registry.evaluate` implementation has no try/except around `spec.compute(context)`, so an exception in any single reader would abort the entire CLI invocation rather than emitting an `unavailable` entry for that metric.
findings.location: ## Approach — context object / emit paragraph; src/rebar/metrics/registry.py:evaluate()
scenarios: A git reader raises on a bare/shallow repo clone in CI; the CLI exits non-zero with a traceback instead of emitting `unavailable` for that metric.
A sidecar reader raises on a missing file; the output map is incomplete, failing AC1's superset assertion.
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan's Approach section add: 'If `compute` raises, `metrics.py` (or `evaluate`) catches the exception and yields `Unavailable(reason=str(exc), accruing_since=spec.accruing_since)` — a reader failure is isolated, never fatal.' Then wrap `spec.compute(context)` in a try/except in `evaluate`.

findings.finding: The plan specifies that each metric emits 'value-or-unavailable' but does not define where/how reader exceptions are caught — if a reader raises, it is unspecified whether `evaluate` catches it and converts it to `unavailable`, or whether it propagates and crashes the CLI process.
findings.location: Approach section / AC line: 'each entry being either a value (with source+confidence) or an unavailable object'
findings.suggested_fix: In `registry.evaluate`, wrap each `spec.compute(ctx)` in a try/except, returning `{"unavailable": true, "reason": str(e)}` on failure. Document this contract in the plan's Approach section and add an AC asserting that a reader that raises still yields an `unavailable` entry rather than propagating.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the golden regeneration list to include ALL five overview-containing snapshots: `__overview_noargs.out`, `__overview_help.out`, `__overview_dashhelp.out`, `__overview_dashh.out`, and `__unknown__.out` (all embed the full subcommand list).
evidence: `tests/interfaces/facades/test_cli_argparse.py` _OVERVIEW_FORMS includes `__overview_noargs`, `__overview_help`, `__overview_dashhelp`, `__overview_dashh`, `__unknown__`, `__help_unknown__`.
The `__overview_noargs` and `__overview_dashh` forms both print the full overview (src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py lines 513-515, 523-524).
`__unknown__` form also writes `_help.overview()` to stdout (line 535).
All five contain the subcommand listing that will change when `metrics` is added.
findings.finding: The plan only names two overview golden snapshots to regenerate (`__overview_help.out`, `__overview_dashhelp.out`) but the full overview text appears in at least three additional golden files (`__overview_noargs.out`, `__overview_dashh.out`, `__unknown__.out`) that also encode the full subcommand list and must be regenerated.
findings.location: Files section: `tests/golden/cli_help/*.out — regenerate the overview snapshots (__overview_help.out, __overview_dashhelp.out)`
scenarios: Implementer regenerates only the two named snapshots; the test parametrize loop also tests `__overview_noargs`, `__overview_dashh`, and `__unknown__`, all of which now mismatch the regenerated overview — the golden suite fails.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Files section, change the golden entry to: `tests/golden/cli_help/*.out — regenerate ALL overview-containing snapshots: __overview_noargs.out, __overview_help.out, __overview_dashhelp.out, __overview_dashh.out, __unknown__.out, plus add metrics__help.out and metrics__dashhelp.out.`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the plan that `metrics` is dispatched via a named intercept arm (`if sub == 'metrics':`) analogous to `grounding-info`, not via any existing frozenset, and that `test_help_overview_coverage.py`'s `_routable_subcommands()` helper must be updated to include `'metrics'`.
evidence: src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py:39-83 — existing frozensets: _READS_INIT_ONLY, _READS_NO_INIT, _FIELD_READS, _LOOKUPS, _DESCENDANTS, _GATES, _SIGNING, _LIFECYCLE, _COMPACT, _BRIDGE, _IO, _WRITES_FULL — none fits a metrics command
src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py:321-324 — `grounding-info` (closest analogue: a repo-independent read) is dispatched via `if sub == 'grounding-info':` named intercept, not any frozenset
tests/interfaces/contracts/test_help_overview_coverage.py:30-62 — `_routable_subcommands()` union of all frozensets + individually-named arms; test enforces routable == known_subcommands()
Plan: 'register the metrics read subcommand in the command-set frozensets and dispatch' — does not name which frozenset
findings.finding: The plan says 'register the `metrics` read subcommand in the command-set frozensets' but the existing dispatch architecture enforces a three-way invariant tested by `test_help_overview_coverage.py`: every subcommand must be (a) in a routable frozenset OR an explicit if-arm listed in `_routable_subcommands()`, (b) have a `help/<sub>.txt` file, AND (c) appear in `overview.txt`. The plan addresses (b) and (c) explicitly, but for (a) it only says 'frozensets' without specifying which one — and the analogous read-only command `grounding-info` is actually dispatched via a named intercept arm (`if sub == 'grounding-info'`), NOT via any frozenset. Placing `metrics` in the wrong frozenset (e.g. `_READS_INIT_ONLY`, which calls `reads.main`) would route it to the wrong handler.
findings.location: Files section — `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py` — 'register the metrics read subcommand in the command-set frozensets and dispatch'
scenarios: Developer adds `metrics` to `_READS_INIT_ONLY` — it routes to `reads.main(['metrics', ...])` which has no metrics arm and prints 'unknown read subcommand'
Developer adds a named intercept arm but forgets to add `metrics` to `_routable_subcommands()` in the test — `test_routable_set_matches_pinned_help_set` fails at CI

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace `docs/cli-reference.md — document the command` in the Files section with `scripts/gen_cli_reference.py — no edit needed (metrics is help-backed); regenerate `docs/cli-reference.md` via `python scripts/gen_cli_reference.py` after adding `help/metrics.txt`.
evidence: docs/cli-reference.md line 3: '**Generated by `scripts/gen_cli_reference.py` — do not edit by hand.**'
scripts/gen_cli_reference.py lines 143-153: raises ValueError if INTERCEPT_COMMANDS drifts from the intercept ladder; CI drift gate fails on stale file.
Plan says 'docs/cli-reference.md — document the command' without mentioning regeneration or the generator script.

`metrics` will be help-backed (has `help/metrics.txt`), so `gen_cli_reference.py` auto-includes it — but `docs/cli-reference.md` must be regenerated, not hand-edited, and the plan's edit set omits the script.
findings.finding: `docs/cli-reference.md` is auto-generated by `scripts/gen_cli_reference.py` (which has a CI drift gate), but the plan lists it as a direct hand-edit target and omits `scripts/gen_cli_reference.py` from the edit set entirely.
findings.location: Files section: `docs/cli-reference.md — document the command`
scenarios: An implementer hand-edits docs/cli-reference.md; CI's drift gate runs `python scripts/gen_cli_reference.py --check`, which overwrites the hand edit and the gate fails.
If the implementer forgets to regenerate, CI fails with 'docs/cli-reference.md is stale'.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Files section, replace the `docs/cli-reference.md` entry with: `docs/cli-reference.md — regenerated (not hand-edited) by running `python scripts/gen_cli_reference.py` after the help file is added; the CI drift gate enforces this.`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the Approach section that `metrics.py` resolves `repo_root` via `rebar.config.repo_root()` (the same resolver used by every other command in the codebase) and passes that string to the context — not `os.getcwd()` or a hardcoded path.
evidence: `git_metrics.module_size_distribution` reads `Path(repo_root) / '.github/module-size-limit.txt'` — must be the actual git root.
`event_metrics._ticket_dirs` calls `str(tracker_dir(repo_root))` from `rebar.config` — must resolve the tracker subdirectory.
All other CLI commands that need repo_root use `config.repo_root()` (e.g., `_cli/_llm_eval_commands.py:49`, `_commands/identity.py:37`, `_commands/session_log.py:44`).
ABSENCE: the plan's Approach section does not mention `config.repo_root()` or how the context's `repo_root` field is populated.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that `metrics.py` constructs a context carrying `repo_root` ('the resolved store root') but never states how `metrics.py` resolves that root. All registered readers depend on this being the correct git-working-tree root: `git_metrics` scans `<repo_root>/src/rebar/**/*.py` and reads `<repo_root>/.github/module-size-limit.txt`; `event_metrics` calls `rebar.config.tracker_dir(repo_root)` to locate the ticket store. Without specifying the resolution mechanism (e.g., `config.repo_root()`, a `--repo-root` flag, or CWD), an implementer may use an incorrect value that silently produces empty/wrong results.
findings.location: Approach section: 'metrics.py constructs a small context (a dataclass or SimpleNamespace) carrying exactly repo_root (the resolved store root), since, and until'
scenarios: Implementer uses `os.getcwd()` as `repo_root`: works from the project root but fails from any subdirectory or in CI where cwd is not the git root.
Implementer omits repo_root resolution entirely (passes `None`): `git_metrics` gets `None` as `repo_root`, causing a `TypeError` in `Path(None)`.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Approach's context object paragraph: 'repo_root is resolved via `str(rebar.config.repo_root())` — the same call every other CLI command uses — so REBAR_ROOT env overrides and git-root detection work consistently.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the bucket key format in the plan (e.g., 'ISO week-start date YYYY-MM-DD for day/week, YYYY-MM for month') and tighten AC5's assertion to match (e.g., `assert any(re.match(r'\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}', k) for k in output['buckets'])`)
evidence: 'For `--bucket`, it builds one context per bucket sub-range and evaluates the registry per bucket, keying the output by bucket' — key format not stated
'CLI test asserts the JSON contains at least one bucket key' — no key-format anchor in the assertion
findings.finding: The bucketed JSON output schema is unspecified: the plan says 'keying the output by bucket' but does not define what the bucket key looks like (e.g., ISO week-start date '2026-01-05', week label '2026-W01', or month '2026-01'). AC5 only asserts 'at least one bucket key' without anchoring the key format, leaving the executor to invent a convention that downstream consumers (and the test assertion) would silently diverge on.
findings.location: Approach section ('For --bucket, it builds one context per bucket sub-range … keying the output by bucket') and AC5
scenarios: An executor emits '2026-W01' as keys; a downstream consumer (or sibling ticket) expects '2026-01-05'; the AC passes but the contract is wrong.
The test fixture uses jq to check for a key but picks the wrong format, making the AC trivially green against an incompatible schema.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the Approach: 'Bucket keys are the ISO 8601 start-date of the sub-range: YYYY-MM-DD for day and week buckets, YYYY-MM for month buckets.' Update AC5 to assert `all(re.match(r'\d{4}-\d{2}(-\d{2})?$', k) for k in output['buckets'].keys())`.

evidence: 2 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
vague/subjective terms present: etc.
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the exact bucket-mode JSON schema in the Output formats section and make AC 5's assertion reference that schema explicitly (e.g. `{"2026-W01": {"since":…,"until":…,"metrics":{…}}, …}`).
evidence: Approach: 'it builds one context per bucket sub-range and evaluates the registry per bucket, keying the output by bucket'
Output formats: JSON is '{"since","until","metrics": {<id>: {…}}}' — a flat single-range shape with no bucket variant
AC 5: 'CLI test asserts the JSON contains at least one bucket key' — implies a different top-level shape but does not define it
ABSENCE: no section defines the bucket-mode JSON schema (e.g. {<bucket_key>: {since, until, metrics:{...}}})
findings.finding: The bucket-mode JSON output shape is inconsistent across three sections: Approach says output is 'keyed by bucket', Output formats defines only a flat schema ({since, until, metrics:{...}}), and AC 5 only checks 'at least one bucket key' — no section defines what the bucket-keyed JSON actually looks like, leaving the implementer without a consistent contract.
findings.location: Approach (--bucket paragraph) vs Output formats (JSON schema) vs AC 5
scenarios: Implementer produces a flat JSON with a 'buckets' sub-key; tester expects top-level bucket keys — AC 5 ambiguously passes or fails
A downstream consumer parses bucket output and finds a shape inconsistent with the flat schema also documented
findings.suggested_fix: Add a bucket-mode schema block to Output formats: `{"<bucket_key>": {"since": "…", "until": "…", "metrics": {<id>: …}}, …}` and update AC 5 to assert `set(output.keys()) == {expected_bucket_keys}` rather than 'at least one bucket key'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the bucketed JSON output schema explicitly in the Output formats section — specify the bucket key format (e.g. ISO 8601 week `'YYYY-Www'`, or sub-range `'YYYY-MM-DD/YYYY-MM-DD'`) and show the top-level shape (`{"buckets": {"<key>": {"since","until","metrics":{...}}, ...}}` or equivalent).
evidence: Plan: 'For `--bucket`, it builds one context per bucket sub-range and evaluates the registry per bucket, keying the output by bucket.'
Plan JSON schema defines only the flat form: `{"since","until","metrics": {<id>: ...}}`
AC5 only checks 'at least one bucket key' exists — it does not constrain key format
No example bucket key string is given anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The bucketed JSON output schema (produced by `--bucket day|week|month`) is unspecified: the plan says the output is 'keyed by bucket' but never defines what the bucket key string looks like (e.g. ISO week `'2026-W03'`, date-range `'2026-01-17/2026-01-24'`, or sub-range start `'2026-01-17'`). The flat-range schema is precisely defined; the bucketed variant is not.
findings.location: ## Approach — `--bucket` paragraph; ## Acceptance Criteria — AC5
scenarios: The implementer and a downstream consumer independently choose different key formats (e.g. ISO week vs. date-range string), producing incompatible JSON.
The CLI test for AC5 passes with any non-empty key, masking a key-format inconsistency.

findings.finding: The bucket key format and per-bucket JSON value schema are undefined: the plan says output is 'keyed by bucket' but never specifies the key string format (YYYY-MM-DD? YYYY-Www? YYYY-MM?) or whether each bucket value is a full `{metric_id: value_or_unavailable}` map.
findings.location: Approach section: 'it builds one context per bucket sub-range and evaluates the registry per bucket, keying the output by bucket'; AC5: 'the JSON contains at least one bucket key'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a concrete example to the Approach: e.g., `{"buckets": {"2026-W01": {"metrics": {"churn": {"value": ..., "source": ..., "confidence": ...}}}, ...}}` with bucket keys as ISO week/month/date strings for the respective bucket size.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define (or explicitly exclude) the text output format for `--bucket` mode, e.g., 'bucketed text emits a `--- bucket: <key> ---` header before each metric block, or is not supported (error with a message).'
evidence: '`--output text` is a deterministic human summary — one line per metric in registry order' — defined only for the non-bucketed case
No mention of `--bucket` + `--output text` behavior anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The `--output text` format for bucketed output (`--bucket` + `--output text`) is entirely unspecified. The plan defines the non-bucketed text layout ('one line per metric … <id> [<lens>/<source>] <value-or-unavailable>') but says nothing about how text mode renders a per-bucket series.
findings.location: Approach section — 'Output formats' paragraph
scenarios: An executor must invent the bucketed text layout (header per bucket? repeated blocks?), causing inconsistency with future tooling or docs.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the non-happy-path input handling for `rebar metrics`: specify validation of `--since`/`--until` ISO format (exit 1 + message on bad format), enforce `since <= until` (exit 1 + message if inverted), clarify whether both flags are required or have sensible defaults, and specify behavior when `--bucket` granularity exceeds the range.
evidence: Plan specifies parsing '--since/--until and optional --bucket day|week|month' but gives no validation rules
No mention of what exit code or message the CLI emits for '--since 2026-13-01' (invalid month) or '--since 2026-07-01 --until 2026-01-01' (inverted range)
Tests cover only the happy path (valid ISO dates, exit 0); no test for bad input
'--since/--until' are mentioned as parsed but it is unspecified whether they are required positional/optional args with or without defaults
findings.finding: The plan does not address what happens when a user provides invalid `--since`/`--until` values (e.g., malformed date strings, a `--since` that is after `--until`, or a range too narrow for the requested `--bucket`), nor whether `--since`/`--until` are required or have defaults. There are no specified error messages or exit codes for these invalid-input cases.
findings.location: Approach — '--bucket day|week|month splits the [since, until] range into sub-ranges'; AC — 'rebar metrics --since 2026-01-01 --until 2026-07-01'
scenarios: User runs `rebar metrics --since not-a-date`; CLI may crash with a Python traceback rather than a clean error message
User runs `rebar metrics --since 2026-07-01 --until 2026-01-01`; inverted range silently produces empty output or crashes the bucketing logic
User omits --since and --until; CLI behavior (default range? error?) is undefined

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a closed `LENSES` frozenset (or equivalent enum) in `registry.py` enumerating the allowed `lens` values, and reference it in the JSON output schema definition in the plan — mirroring the treatment of `SOURCES` and `CONFIDENCES`.
evidence: Plan output schema: `{"lens","source","confidence","value"}`
registry.py defines `SOURCES: frozenset` and `CONFIDENCES: frozenset` but no `LENSES` frozenset
MetricSpec.lens is typed `str` with no constraint
Live lens values: 'code_health', 'delivery', 'agent_process', 'gate_economics' — ad hoc, never declared as a closed set
findings.finding: The `lens` field appears in the machine-contract JSON output schema (`{"lens","source","confidence","value"}`) but has no closed vocabulary defined anywhere — unlike `source` (SOURCES frozenset) and `confidence` (CONFIDENCES frozenset), which are enumerated. Existing registered values ('code_health', 'delivery', 'agent_process', 'gate_economics') are never declared as the full allowed set.
findings.location: ## Approach — Output formats section; src/rebar/metrics/registry.py MetricSpec.lens field
scenarios: A consumer of the JSON output (e.g. a dashboard) cannot validate or enumerate the lens dimension without a defined vocabulary.
A new metric author picks an arbitrary lens string that silently diverges from the schema contract.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `LENSES: frozenset[str] = frozenset({"code_health", "delivery", "agent_process", "gate_economics"})` to `registry.py` alongside `SOURCES`/`CONFIDENCES`, and note it in the Output formats section of the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend an existing one) that verifies `docs/cli-reference.md` was updated, e.g., `grep -q '\-\-since' docs/cli-reference.md && grep -q 'metrics' docs/cli-reference.md` exits 0.
evidence: 'docs/cli-reference.md — document the command' listed in Files
No AC in the Acceptance Criteria section references docs/cli-reference.md
findings.finding: `docs/cli-reference.md` is listed in the Files edit set but has no corresponding acceptance criterion that verifies it was actually updated — no AC checks its content, and no proving command references it.
findings.location: Files section — 'docs/cli-reference.md — document the command'
scenarios: The docs file is skipped or left stale; all ACs pass green; the deliverable is silently incomplete.
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: '`grep -E "rebar metrics|--since|--until" docs/cli-reference.md` exits 0 — confirms the reference doc was updated.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion verifying that `docs/cli-reference.md` is updated (e.g. assert it contains `rebar metrics` and `--since`/`--until` flags, either as a file-content check or a doc-build lint step).
evidence: Files: 'docs/cli-reference.md — document the command'
ABSENCE: no AC references docs/cli-reference.md or asserts its content
findings.finding: The deliverable `docs/cli-reference.md` is listed in the Files section but no acceptance criterion verifies that it is updated — it is an orphaned deliverable.
findings.location: Files section ('docs/cli-reference.md — document the command') vs Acceptance Criteria (no covering AC)
scenarios: Implementation ships without updating the docs file; all ACs pass; the gap is undetected until a user reads stale documentation

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one failure-path AC to `tests/interfaces/test_metrics_cli.py`: assert that `rebar metrics` exits non-zero (or emits a structured error) when given a malformed `--since`/`--until` date, an invalid `--bucket` value, or an inverted date range.
evidence: AC only specifies: 'rebar metrics --since 2026-01-01 --until 2026-07-01 --output json exits 0'
No AC or test description mentions error-exit behavior, error message content, or any invalid-input scenario
Plan states 'Read-only' and 'CLI (real subprocess against a temp store)' but lists only success-path assertions
findings.finding: The testing plan covers only the happy path for the new user-facing CLI flow; no tests are specified for invalid or malformed inputs such as non-ISO `--since`/`--until` values, inverted date ranges (`--since` > `--until`), an invalid `--bucket` value (e.g. `--bucket hour`), or an unknown `--output` format — all standard failure paths for a date-range CLI command.
findings.location: Tests section and Acceptance Criteria
scenarios: A user passes `--since not-a-date` and the command silently emits broken JSON or panics instead of exiting non-zero with a clear error
A user passes `--bucket hour` (not in `day|week|month`) and gets an unspecified behavior
A user passes `--since 2026-07-01 --until 2026-01-01` (inverted range) and gets an empty or misleading result
findings.suggested_fix: Add ACs such as: (a) `rebar metrics --since bad-date --until 2026-07-01` exits non-zero and stderr contains a date-parse error; (b) `rebar metrics --bucket hour` exits non-zero with an 'invalid bucket' message; (c) `rebar metrics --since 2026-07-01 --until 2026-01-01` either exits non-zero or emits an empty-but-valid `metrics` map with a warning.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AGENTS.md entry (or ADR) capturing the adapter-isolation / portability-guard constraint — why it exists, what it prevents, and how it is enforced — so the rule is discoverable independently of commit history.
evidence: "metrics.py reaches metrics only through rebar.metrics.registry … it must NOT import any rebar.metrics.adapters module"
"the portability guard from 1f77/538c" — references prior commit context that is not surfaced in any plan-listed doc
No AGENTS.md, no ADR file, no design-doc is listed in the Files section
findings.finding: The portability guard (adapter isolation) is a novel cross-component architectural rule — 'metrics.py must NOT import rebar.metrics.adapters' — enforced by a source-level test, but no ADR, AGENTS.md entry, or design-doc update is listed in the file set. Future contributors have no persistent, discoverable record of WHY this constraint exists or how to maintain it.
findings.location: Files list (no ADR/AGENTS.md entry) + Approach section: 'Adapter isolation (portability guard from 1f77/538c)'
scenarios: A contributor adds a new reader or refactors imports and inadvertently introduces a direct adapter import, not knowing the constraint exists beyond a test assertion.
The test is bypassed or the constraint is misunderstood without a durable written rationale.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `AGENTS.md` (or `docs/adr/NNN-metrics-adapter-isolation.md`) to the Files list and include a section explaining: (1) the portability guard rationale, (2) that `_commands/metrics.py` must never import `rebar.metrics.adapters.*`, and (3) that the `test_metrics_cli.py` source-scan assertion enforces this.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that `metrics.py` will use `rebar._engine_support.output.parse_output(argv, 'report')` for `--output json|text` parsing rather than hand-rolling inline flag logic, to reuse the 20+-call-site single-source-of-truth utility.
evidence: 20+ call sites in the codebase use `from rebar._engine_support.output import parse_output` with `parse_output(argv, 'report')` for the text|json flag, including `signing.py`, `bridge.py`, `gates.py`, `validate.py`, `fsck.py`, `idea.py`, `delete.py`, `composer.py`, `transition.py`, `claim.py`, etc.
`output.py` PROFILES already defines `'report': ('text', ('text', 'json'))` — the exact default+allowed set the plan specifies for `metrics`.
Plan is silent on whether `metrics.py` will use `parse_output` or inline its own `--output` parsing.
findings.finding: The plan's `metrics.py` will need to parse `--output json|text` but does not mention using the existing `rebar._engine_support.output.parse_output` utility, which already has a `report` profile (default `text`, allowed `{text, json}`) matching exactly what the `metrics` command needs. There are 20+ existing call-sites across the codebase. Leaving this unspecified risks the implementer hand-rolling inline `--output` parsing — an NIH duplication of a single-source-of-truth utility.
findings.location: Approach section — 'Output formats.' / `src/rebar/_commands/metrics.py`
scenarios: Implementer writes inline while-loop `--output` parsing (as done inconsistently in `_audit_commands.py`), diverging from the 20+ sites that use the single utility and making `metrics` the second non-conforming command.
Future `--output` profile additions or error-message changes in `output.py` do not propagate to `metrics.py`.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the Approach / Output formats section: "`metrics.py` parses `--output` via `from rebar._engine_support.output import parse_output; fmt, rest = parse_output(argv, 'report')` (the `report` profile already provides `default='text', allowed={'text','json'}`)."

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/interfaces/facades/test_cli_argparse.py` to the Files section (or confirm `metrics` will be added to an existing, already-covered frozenset such as `_READS_INIT_ONLY`) and document which frozenset `metrics` belongs to.
evidence: test_cli_argparse.py lines 95–103: `inproc = _READS_INIT_ONLY | _READS_NO_INIT | _WRITES_FULL | _FIELD_READS | _LOOKUPS | _DESCENDANTS | _GATES` — only 7 sets unioned.
test_cli_argparse.py line 118: `assert inproc <= known` — does NOT assert `known <= inproc`, so a known subcommand in a NEW frozenset passes silently.
Plan says 'frozensets' (plural) without naming which one.
`tests/interfaces/facades/test_cli_argparse.py` is absent from the plan's Files section.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify which frozenset in `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py` `metrics` belongs to, and `tests/interfaces/facades/test_cli_argparse.py::test_routing_tables_cover_every_known_subcommand` only unions 7 specific frozensets (`_READS_INIT_ONLY`, `_READS_NO_INIT`, `_WRITES_FULL`, `_FIELD_READS`, `_LOOKUPS`, `_DESCENDANTS`, `_GATES`). If `metrics` is placed in a new frozenset, that test's coverage check silently skips it, and the test file itself is an unenumerated consumer requiring update.
findings.location: Files section: `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py — register the metrics read subcommand in the command-set frozensets and dispatch`; `tests/interfaces/facades/test_cli_argparse.py` lines 95–118
scenarios: Implementer creates a new `_METRICS` frozenset for `metrics`; the routing coverage test doesn't include it in `inproc`, so the disjoint-ness and coverage assertions don't validate the new arm.
A typo in the frozenset name goes undetected because the test doesn't cover it.

findings.finding: docs/cli-reference.md is listed as a deliverable in the Files section but no acceptance criterion verifies it was created or populated correctly — it is orphaned work with no covering AC.
findings.location: Files section: 'docs/cli-reference.md — document the command.' / Acceptance Criteria (all five)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3: 'and `docs/cli-reference.md` contains a `metrics` section documenting `--since`, `--until`, `--bucket`, and `--output`.'

findings.finding: `--output text` is described in the Approach as a deliverable ('a human summary, mirrors rebar audit') but no acceptance criterion verifies it is implemented or produces correct output.
findings.location: Approach section ('--output text a human summary') vs. Acceptance Criteria
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: '`rebar metrics --since 2026-01-01 --until 2026-07-01 --output text` exits 0 and stdout contains at least one metric identifier (human-readable summary is non-empty).'

findings.finding: The `--output text` summary format is undefined: the plan says it 'mirrors rebar audit' but the audit text renderer is structurally inapplicable to metric data, leaving field names, ordering, and unavailable presentation unspecified.
findings.location: Approach section: 'JSON is the default stdout contract, `--output text` a human summary (mirrors `rebar audit`)'

findings.finding: The portability guard conflates `rebar.metrics` (the package __init__, which safely populates REGISTRY by importing the reader modules) with `rebar.metrics.adapters.*` (the optional CI adapters). Restricting `metrics.py` to only import `rebar.metrics.registry` means the `register()` side-effects in `event_metrics.py`, `git_metrics.py`, and `sidecar_metrics.py` never fire, leaving REGISTRY at only the 3 seed stubs. The command would silently evaluate and emit only 3 metrics instead of all 12 currently registered ones, and the AC superset assertion (`set(output['metrics'].keys()) >= {s.id for s in REGISTRY}`) passes trivially only if the test also imports from the un-populated `registry` module — otherwise it fails with 3 < 12.
findings.location: Approach section — 'Adapter isolation (portability guard)' paragraph; AC line 1
findings.suggested_fix: Change the portability guard statement to: 'metrics.py imports `rebar.metrics` (triggering registry population) and `rebar.metrics.registry` for the REGISTRY/evaluate symbols; it must NOT directly import any `rebar.metrics.adapters.*` submodule. The no-adapters-import test should assert the absence of `rebar.metrics.adapters` in the import, not the absence of `rebar.metrics`.' The AC test should then import REGISTRY via `from rebar.metrics import REGISTRY` to get the fully-populated list.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
